brand_description: Original intimate electronic indie-pop/downtempo brand blending soft machine rhythms, warm analog synths, trip-hop shadow, cinematic calm, and fragile romantic melancholy. Inspired by broad indietronica, downtempo, and trip-hop moods without copying any real artist, voice, lyrics, melodies, drum programming, arrangements, production, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Fans of intimate electronic indie pop, glitchy romantic melancholy, downtempo beats, trip-hop atmospheres, soft vocal hooks, late-night headphone music, cinematic ambient texture, and restrained songs that still land as memorable pop.
audience.guardrail: General streaming audience, 16+. Melancholy, loneliness, longing, romantic distance, memory, insomnia, urban isolation, and emotional ambiguity are allowed. Avoid explicit sexual detail, graphic violence, hate, slurs, self-harm instruction, drug glorification, or child-directed framing.

character.name: Afterimage Relay
character.core_concept: A quiet transmission of late-night messages, soft machines, remembered rooms, and fragile human feeling moving through glowing wires.
character.fallback_summary: intimate electronic indie-pop artist blending glitchy warmth, downtempo melancholy, trip-hop shadow, and soft melodic hooks
personality_traits: Tender and emotionally restrained rather than melodramatic
Romantic but distant, as if singing through a signal delay
Comfortable moving between soft electronic pop and darker downtempo atmosphere
character.visual_identity: Minimal nocturnal indie-electronic artwork with soft cream or dark-blue space, blurred city lights, signal lines, paper texture, small glowing shapes, and quiet analog-machine details. Visuals should feel like late-night postcards, old answering machines, train windows, waveform traces, and cinematic haze.
visual_reference: Muted night palette with soft blue, cream, amber, charcoal, and pale pink
Subtle waveform, envelope, relay, postcard, wire, and window motifs used occasionally, not forced
Blurred city lights, train windows, rainy glass, room lamps, and quiet streets
Analog synths, tape machines, answering machines, and small LEDs as background texture
Minimal indie sleeve design with restrained typography and no crowded text

music.default_style: intimate electronic indie pop with downtempo, trip-hop, ambient, and soft synth-pop textures
music.default_prompt: Original intimate electronic indie pop with delicate vocal melodies, precise programmed drums, warm analog synths, subtle glitch percussion, airy pads, downtempo bass, and cinematic late-night atmosphere. Vary the lane by song: soft romantic electronic pop, glitchy drum-machine confessionals, darker trip-hop grooves, ambient piano/synth laments, warm nostalgic synth-pop, and sparse nocturnal instrumentals with vocal fragments are all valid. Never imitate, clone, or reference any real artist voice, cadence, lyrics, melody, drum pattern, arrangement, production, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with intimacy, restraint, melancholy, warmth, shadow, and memorable hooks.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: long-distance intimacy, messages sent too late, memory and regret, quiet city nights, love becoming static, loneliness inside technology, fragile optimism, dreamlike travel, emotional distance, and recovery through small private rituals. Do not repeat the same signal, mail, porcelain, machine, rain, or night imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with a quiet unresolved image, soft repeated phrase, fading question, intimate confession, or cinematic afterglow; avoid tidy moral lessons or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original songs that feel intimate, melodic, textural, restrained, and quietly devastating, with enough variation to support albums without repeating one soft-electronic formula.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Flexible first-person, direct address, or close observational narration. The narrator can feel tender, distant, regretful, hopeful, numb, romantic, or quietly haunted depending on the song lane.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person or second-person, with occasional cinematic third-person distance.
character_voice.tone: Soft, intimate, restrained, lonely, precise, romantic, and quietly surreal; never melodramatic or derivative.
character_voice.vocabulary: Plain emotional language with small tactile details, digital-age intimacy, urban images, and concise poetic turns.
character_voice.comedy_mode: None; occasional dry understatement is allowed but humor should not become a defining feature.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics should be appropriate for teens and adults.

reference_artists_for_internal_vibe_only: The Postal Service Give Up-era intimate glitch-pop melancholy
Portishead-era trip-hop shadow and noir atmosphere
songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, lyrics, melodies, drum programming, synth motifs, sample style, arrangements, album sequencing, persona, or copyrighted material. Use the broad emotional and sonic range as inspiration for an original Afterimage Relay identity.
allowed_elements: intimate soft vocal melodies with subtle harmonies
precise programmed drums and glitch percussion
warm analog synth chords and ambient pads
romantic distance, late messages, memory, and quiet regret
cinematic piano, bells, or soft electric keys
minimal hooks that grow through texture instead of volume
abstract digital imagery anchored by plain emotional lines
clean or radio-friendly language by default
space and silence as part of the arrangement
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist vocal style, melody, drum pattern, synth motif, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
overusing the same title examples, signal imagery, mail imagery, porcelain imagery, or invented lore terms
glorified drug abuse or instructions for drug use
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Hooks should be understated but memorable; every song should invent its own hook language.
Use at least one concrete sensory, room, travel, or object detail in each song to prevent generic melancholy.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be sung or spoken.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Avoid explicit content unless a separate release profile explicitly permits it.
Emotional darkness is allowed, but the song must not instruct or encourage self-harm, dangerous behavior, or substance abuse.
song_family_types: Intimate glitch-pop confession with precise drums, soft vocal hook, and romantic distance.
Cinematic downtempo instrumental-pop hybrid with piano, pads, sparse lyrics, and calm melancholy.
Dark trip-hop nocturne with heavy low-end, smoky atmosphere, and restrained vocal tension.
Warm nostalgic synth-pop song with brighter chorus, bittersweet lyric, and gentle pulse.
Sparse late-night room song with minimal beat, close vocal, and unresolved ending.
Dream-travel vignette with ambient texture, soft rhythm, and a hook that feels like a memory.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate drum feel: crisp glitch-pop programming, downtempo breakbeat, half-time trip-hop, soft four-on-the-floor pulse, brushed minimal percussion, or near-beatless ambient drift.
Rotate harmonic bed: analog synth pad, piano loop, electric keys, muted guitar texture, low drone, or bell-like motif.
Rotate vocal shape: close single vocal, hushed double, airy harmonies, chopped vocal texture, call-back refrain, or nearly spoken intimate verse.
Rotate texture: clean digital intimacy, tape-worn warmth, rain-glass haze, noir low-end, bright synth-pop shimmer, or ambient blur.
Use occasional instrumental breaks, but not every song; breaks should feel like emotional space, not filler.
tempo_energy_lanes: 64-76 BPM: very slow downtempo lament, cinematic calm, sparse lyric density.
77-90 BPM: trip-hop shadow, heavy low-end, restrained vocal tension.
91-104 BPM: intimate glitch-pop pulse, soft hook, balanced verse and chorus.
105-118 BPM: brighter synth-pop movement, bittersweet but more kinetic.
119-128 BPM: rare dance-adjacent release, still intimate and emotionally shaded.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: vocal fragment, drum-machine pickup, ambient pad, piano motif, field-like texture, or immediate bass pulse.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, beat feel, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
Do not always build to a bigger final chorus; sometimes end smaller, blurrier, darker, or nearly instrumental.
Use glitches and electronic artifacts as emotional punctuation, not constant decoration.
Avoid identical verse lengths, chorus density, bridge placement, and outro fades across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: soft sung chorus, tiny repeated refrain, melodic question, whispered callback, chopped vocal motif, or instrumental hook.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, title placement, or melodic contour across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: longing, regret, numbness, tenderness, distance, surrender, or fragile hope.
Use repetition for intimacy, but change harmony, texture, silence, or lyric detail on later returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: unsent message, room memory, train-window scene, late-night confession, detached observer, dream logic, or direct romantic address.
Do not make every song about messages, technology, or distance; those are lanes, not the whole brand.
When lyrics become abstract, include one plain human sentence that clarifies the emotional center.
When lyrics become romantic, avoid cliché and include a specific object, place, or small action.
When lyrics become dark, keep the tone restrained and avoid glamorizing collapse.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same signal, relay, mail, machine, porcelain, rain, train, window, or night imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, symbols, objects, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as waking up, checking a phone, hearing rain, riding a train, or staring through glass.
Do not reuse the same chorus cadence, drum pattern, synth motif, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Afterimage Relay identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with an accessible electronic-pop track that establishes intimacy and texture without using the darkest lane first.
Sequence brighter glitch-pop tracks beside trip-hop or ambient pieces so the project breathes.
Use one or two nearly instrumental or minimal tracks as atmosphere pivots, not filler.
Place the most emotionally direct song after a more textural or detached track for contrast.
Save the darkest trip-hop or most cinematic downtempo track for the late-middle of the album.
End with either a soft unresolved refrain, a calm instrumental afterimage, or a quiet human line over fading texture.
earworm_stack: Introduce a memorable synth, vocal, piano, or drum-machine motif within the first 15 seconds.
Pair delicate verses with a simple emotional refrain.
Let silence, texture, and small changes create replay value.
Use one vivid object or room detail per section rather than crowding the lyric with symbols.
Keep hooks memorable without making them overly obvious or chant-like.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be soft, memorable, emotionally precise, or texturally unforgettable.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse intimacy and hook openness.
For trip-hop lanes, let bass and atmosphere carry part of the hook; for glitch-pop lanes, let rhythm and melody carry it.

energy_arc.pattern: GLOW -> PULSE -> MESSAGE -> DISTANCE -> RETURN -> AFTERIMAGE
rules: Start with a recognizable texture, drum-machine figure, synth shape, piano motif, or close vocal idea.
Build emotional intimacy through restraint before adding layers.
Reveal the plain emotional truth before the final third.
Let the final section shift energy: warmer, darker, smaller, blurrier, or more open depending on the song family.
End with a memorable afterimage, not a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Soft refrain listeners can hum quietly without dense lyrics
A tiny repeated phrase used as emotional anchor, not slogan
Instrumental motif that returns like a remembered message
Final line that reframes the song emotionally
Subtle backing vocal texture used sparingly for lift
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Postal Service, Moby, or Portishead imitation rather than an original adjacent genre/emotional lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, beat feel, synth texture, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
If a song is very soft, confirm it still has a memorable hook or motif.
If a song is dark or trip-hop leaning, confirm it still has human warmth somewhere.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: This character's vocal is irreplaceable because it sits in the narrow register between confession and transmission — close enough to feel private, distant enough to feel like a signal arriving from somewhere you can no longer reach.
vocal_textures: A clean, slightly airy mid-register tone that sounds like it was recorded in a small lit room at 2am, with gentle room presence but no reverb wash
Syllables clipped at the ends as though the message was cut short before the emotion could become melodramatic
Occasional breathy onset on stressed words that makes the line feel physically close, like the microphone is inside the thought
A flat, almost spoken delivery on the most painful lines, where pitch barely moves but emotional weight is entirely in the word choice and timing
Soft doubling of specific phrases where one vocal layer is slightly behind the other, creating the feel of a memory overlapping the present moment
Very controlled vibrato used sparingly — only at the end of a phrase that earns release — never as a constant feature
timing_behaviors: Deliver certain syllables a half-beat late, as though the narrator hesitated before admitting the word, then catches up cleanly on the next phrase
Leave a small silence before the title or hook word rather than hitting it on the exact downbeat, letting the beat breathe around it
On drum-machine tracks, occasionally push against the grid slightly on emotional peaks to make programmed precision feel human and fragile
When a verse has long, stretched phrases, interrupt with one short clipped phrase to reset intimacy and prevent drift
Allow the final syllable of a section to decay slightly under the beat rather than landing with conviction, signaling emotional incompletion
adlib_behaviors: Add a near-inaudible exhale or soft voiced breath between lines that functions as punctuation, not performance
Occasionally repeat the last word of a phrase at lower volume under the next line, as though the narrator is still processing what was just said
Use a single wordless hum or tonal syllable in the drop or bridge — not a flourish, but a signal that language has temporarily run out
Layer a whispered version of the hook under the sung version in the final section, making the hook feel like it is fading into memory
Resist filling silence with ad-libs; when the track leaves space, the voice waits — emptiness is an adlib choice here
avoid: Melisma, runs, or vocal acrobatics — any display of technique breaks the transmission-from-a-quiet-room illusion
Shouted or belted chorus peaks — emotional intensity here is expressed through restraint, not volume
Warm, breathy pop-smooth production sheen — the vocal should feel lightly processed and slightly exposed, not polished to a mainstream finish
Narrator as wounded performer — the voice should feel like someone sending a message, not someone performing grief for an audience
Aggressive vibrato or sustained held notes that signal theatrical emotion rather than private feeling

performance_conceit_bank: The narrator sings the entire verse as though reading aloud from something written down — slightly more rhythmically even than natural speech — then breaks that controlled cadence on the last line, where the thought becomes too direct to perform.

A second vocal layer enters mid-chorus and sings a different clause of the same sentence simultaneously, so the hook contains two grammatical halves that never quite finish each other — the listener hears completion but cannot identify which line did it.

The voice drops to near-spoken level for exactly one line in the bridge that states the plainest emotional fact of the song, surrounded by more melodic material, making the plain line land harder than any held note could.

On a glitch-pop track, the vocal is split so that consonants are slightly time-shifted from vowels through subtle editing, making the voice feel like a signal that has traveled a long distance and arrived slightly disassembled but still legible.

The hook is sung the first time at low volume and incomplete phrasing, as though the narrator is testing whether to say it at all, then repeated at full delivery only in the final section — the emotional payoff depends on having withheld it.

In a slow downtempo track, the narrator does not sing the last line of the final chorus — only the instrumental plays where the lyric should be, leaving the listener to complete it internally from prior repetition.

A recurring two-note melodic cell — the same two pitches — appears in the verse, the hook, and once in the outro at different rhythms and harmonies, acting as a sonic through-line the listener may not consciously notice but will feel as coherence.

The narrator addresses the same subject using second person in the verse, first person in the chorus, and third person in the bridge — not as a stylistic shift but as the emotional mechanism of the song: moving from direct address to identification to release.

album_mode_lanes.name: Glitch Confession
album_mode_lanes.description: Programmed drums run clean and precise while the vocal sits slightly off the grid, creating tension between machine certainty and human hesitation; the lyric is direct and personal, structured like a message that became a song by accident; glitch artifacts appear once or twice as emotional punctuation, not as aesthetic decoration

album_mode_lanes.name: Slow Signal
album_mode_lanes.description: The tempo drops below 76 BPM and the track breathes almost entirely in space; vocals are minimal and close-mic'd, piano or bell-tone motifs do melodic work the voice does not need to do, and the lyric is spare enough that each word carries structural weight; this lane ends before it resolves

album_mode_lanes.name: Night Architecture
album_mode_lanes.description: A fuller arrangement with warm analog pads, a steady drum-machine pulse in the 91-104 BPM range, and a chorus that opens the harmonic space without becoming anthemic; the song has the feeling of moving through a city at night — forward motion with private interior narration underneath

album_mode_lanes.name: Dream Correspondence
album_mode_lanes.description: Ambient textures and soft rhythm elements carry a lyric written in associative logic rather than linear narrative; the hook is melodically simple but harmonically unexpected, resolving to a chord that feels like remembering instead of arriving; this lane permits the most surreal imagery in the catalog

album_mode_lanes.name: Heavy Low Room
album_mode_lanes.description: Trip-hop weight in the bass and kick, smoky pad texture, and a vocal that barely rises above the mix level; the lyric is less concerned with direct address and more with atmosphere and situation; emotional tension is carried by the space between the bass and the voice, not by melodic movement

album_mode_lanes.name: Bittersweet Pulse
album_mode_lanes.description: The brightest lane: tempo moves into the 105-118 BPM range, synths carry melodic hooks the voice can lean against, and the lyric contains something that was lost but is described with warmth rather than pain; this lane is the emotional outlier in a set and should be used rarely enough to function as relief

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous track used a glitch-edited vocal conceit, the next track must use an entirely smooth, unprocessed vocal approach — the character's tendency is to over-apply digital texture when in doubt, so smooth must be a deliberate choice.

If the previous track ended ambiguously or with a fade into texture, the next track must have a clear final moment — a single last note, a final spoken syllable, a drum pattern that stops rather than dissolves — to prevent the album from drifting into one long ending.

If the previous track was primarily atmospheric and lyric-light, the next track must carry a complete narrative arc with a verse, a hook, and a bridge that shifts emotional perspective — this character defaults to texture when structural work feels hard.

Do not allow two consecutive tracks to open with the same type of element: if one opens on a synth pad, the next must open on a drum figure, piano motif, bass pulse, or immediate vocal — the character's default is to open everything on a pad texture.

If the previous track used second-person direct address throughout, the next must use either pure first-person interiority or cinematic third-person distance — the character tends to stay in second-person address as an emotional comfort move.

Ensure that no two adjacent tracks share a tempo within 8 BPM of each other and that their primary emotional postures — longing, numbness, warmth, tension, release — are different named states, not gradations of the same state
anti_generic_rules: This character's specific failure mode is writing a verse full of beautiful small details and then abandoning them in the chorus for a vague emotional declaration — every chorus must contain at least one specific object, action, or place that earns the feeling it carries.

The character defaults to rain, windows, and late-night train imagery when the lyric-writing stalls — if any of these appear, they must earn their place by doing something structurally unusual, not simply signaling atmosphere.

Generic output for this style produces a soft synth pad, a four-on-the-floor kick with light room reverb, and a minor-key vocal that rises on the word 'gone' — if any of these three elements appear together unchallenged, the song must be redesigned.

The character tends to mistake restraint for emptiness — a track that has nothing except quiet is not restrained, it is unfinished; every track must have at least one moment of deliberate sonic choice that could only have been made by this specific character's production sensibility.

When emotional stakes are high the character defaults to abstraction as a protective move — the plainest, most direct sentence in a lyric is usually the most important one, and it must not be buried under metaphor or left out entirely.

The character's programmed drums tend to become decorative over time, running in the background without rhythmic personality — drums must have at least one moment per track where their pattern, gap, or texture is the primary event, not the backdrop.

Hooks that end on the tonic note and land on beat one of the bar are this character's comfort-zone escape hatch — at least half of all hooks should resolve unexpectedly, either harmonically, rhythmically, or by ending mid-phrase.
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not open more than one track per album with a slowly rising synth pad that establishes key and mood before any rhythm or melody enters — this is the character's most-used opener and it must be rationed to one use per project.
Do not use the layered whisper-under-sung-hook technique in more than two songs on a given album — it is a signature moment, not a default texture.

Do not place the bridge at the same structural position — following the second chorus — in more than two consecutive tracks; vary bridge placement to keep the album's architecture from feeling formulaic.

Do not use the same harmonic move — minor chord resolving down to the relative major — as the emotional release point in more than two songs on the same album; find different harmonic ways to deliver warmth or resolution.
Do not repeat the lyrical device of an unsent or incomplete message as the central conceit in back-to-back tracks — message incompleteness is one lane in a larger catalog, not the only lane.

Do not use glitch artifacts as the primary textural event in two tracks that will sit adjacent in a sequence — space them across the album so each glitch moment feels like a chosen event rather than a stylistic tic.

Do not allow the drum-machine pattern on consecutive tracks to share the same kick placement on beat one and snare on beat three — vary the grid relationship of bass and snare to prevent rhythmic sameness from erasing the emotional distinctiveness of each song.

Do not end more than two tracks on the same album with a fade into pad texture; outros must vary across forms — a held single note, a drum pattern stopping cold, a voice alone, an ambient loop that loops visibly — so that each ending feels like a specific choice about that song's meaning.
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify the exact vocal conceit this track uses — what the voice does differently from every other track already planned for this album — and explain why that conceit serves this specific lyric's emotional logic rather than simply varying technique for variety's sake.

The brief must name the single plainest sentence in the lyric — the line where the narrator stops performing and states the emotional fact directly — and confirm that this line is structurally visible rather than buried inside a verse or hidden under production.

The brief must identify which of the character's known failure modes is most likely on this track given its tempo lane and emotional posture, and specify what production or lyrical decision will counteract that failure mode before it emerges.

The brief must describe the outro's specific ending form — not 'fade out' but the precise final element that will be the last thing the listener hears — and explain how that ending creates an afterimage specific to this song rather than a generic dissolve.

The brief must confirm that the track's primary sensory detail — the concrete object, room element, or physical action that anchors the lyric — has not appeared in any other track on this album, and specify what emotional work that detail performs in the hook or verse where it lives.

The brief must state the tempo, the BPM lane it falls within, the drum-machine pattern character in plain terms, and confirm that this combination has not appeared in the immediately preceding track, with a note on how the rhythmic personality of this track differs audibly from the one before it.

visuals.style: Minimal nocturnal electronic-pop artwork with soft grain, muted night colors, blurred lights, waveform traces, relay lines, analog-machine details, lonely room/window imagery, and restrained typography. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit drug imagery, weapons, gore, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words
visuals.text_overlay_style: minimal clean indie sleeve typography, limited words, soft contrast, subtle grain, no dense text blocks

distribution.content_advisory: clean or radio-friendly by default; melancholy and lightly mature themes allowed without explicit content
